attributes.stem:
A 12-day-old boy presents to the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit with 24 hours of poor feeding and lethargy. He was born at 28 weeks gestation and has remained admitted to hospital since birth. His history of presenting complaint is reduced oral intake, decreased activity, and new fever.

His medical history includes respiratory distress syndrome requiring mechanical ventilation. He has a central venous catheter and recently received broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected sepsis. He does not take any regular medications.

There is no relevant travel history. On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 170/min, blood pressure 65/40 mmHg, respiratory rate 60/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 38.2°C. Oral examination shows adherent white plaques consistent with thrush. Other examination findings are unremarkable.

Investigations reveal blood cultures growing Candida albicans. A lumbar puncture and urine culture have not yet been performed. An ophthalmology review and echocardiogram are pending.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following antifungal regimens is the most appropriate initial therapy?
attributes.explanation:
Neonatal candidaemia carries a high risk of dissemination, including central nervous system and ocular involvement. Contemporary recommendations support an echinocandin for initial therapy in neonates because of a favourable safety profile and fewer drug interactions compared with triazoles, provided that central nervous system and urinary tract sources are excluded. Among echinocandins, micafungin has the most neonatal experience and is preferred. In the scenario described, initial management should prioritise rapid, well-tolerated systemic therapy while promptly arranging lumbar puncture, urine studies, and source control (including consideration of central venous catheter removal).

Intravenous micafungin is preferred when meningitis has been excluded and there is no urinary tract source, due to reduced toxicity and minimal interactions. In practice, many centres commence micafungin empirically and proceed urgently to exclude central nervous system and urinary tract involvement; if meningitis is identified or cannot be excluded, a switch to fluconazole is appropriate given its reliable central nervous system penetration.

Intravenous amphotericin is an alternative systemic option and historically first-line; however, it has greater nephrotoxicity and infusion-related adverse effects. Where echinocandins are unavailable or there is concern for species with reduced susceptibility to fluconazole, amphotericin remains reasonable. In a stable neonate with suspected line-associated candidaemia and pending evaluation for central nervous system involvement, micafungin offers a more favourable safety profile.

Oral fluconazole is not appropriate initial therapy for neonatal candidaemia because reliable systemic exposure is uncertain in critically unwell preterm infants; intravenous administration is preferred when fluconazole is indicated.

Oral nystatin and topical miconazole are limited to mucocutaneous disease such as oral thrush and have no role in treating bloodstream infection because they do not achieve therapeutic systemic concentrations.

Key additional steps include prompt lumbar puncture to assess for meningitis, urinalysis and urine culture to evaluate a urinary tract source, ophthalmological examination for ocular seeding, echocardiography where indicated, and removal of the central venous catheter if feasible. If meningitis is present or cannot be excluded, or if a urinary tract source is identified, intravenous fluconazole is a suitable alternative due to superior penetration into cerebrospinal fluid and urine.
learningPoints: Echinocandins are preferred for neonatal candidaemia due to fewer interactions and reduced toxicity when central nervous system and urinary tract sources are excluded.
Fluconazole is appropriate when meningitis is present, has not been excluded, or when a urinary tract source is suspected.
Micafungin is the echinocandin of choice in neonates given growing experience and safety data.
references: Vasileiou E, Apsemidou A, Vyzantiadis TA, Tragiannidis A. Invasive candidiasis and candidemia in pediatric and neonatal patients: A review of current guidelines. Curr Med Mycol. 2018 Sep;4(3):28-33. doi: 10.18502/cmm.4.3.173. PMID: 30619967; PMCID: PMC6315202.

Kilpatrick R, Scarrow E, Hornik C, Greenberg RG. Neonatal invasive candidiasis: updates on clinical management and prevention. Lancet Child Adolesc Health. 2022 Jan;6(1):60-70. doi: 10.1016/S2352-4642(21)00272-8. Epub 2021 Oct 19. PMID: 34672994.

Mora-Duarte J, Betts R, Rotstein C, Colombo AL, Thompson-Moya L, Smietana J, Lupinacci R, Sable C, Kartsonis N, Perfect J; Caspofungin Invasive Candidiasis Study Group. Comparison of caspofungin and amphotericin B for invasive candidiasis. N Engl J Med. 2002 Dec 19;347(25):2020-9. doi: 10.1056/NEJMoa021585. PMID: 12490683.

Kontoyiannis DP, Bodey GP, Mantzoros CS. Fluconazole vs. amphotericin B for the management of candidaemia in adults: a meta-analysis. Mycoses. 2001;44(5):125-35. doi: 10.1046/j.1439-0507.2001.00649.x. PMID: 11486448.
Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic ("Candidaemia (including Candida and related species sepsis)"). eTG. Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd; March 2025.
